title: The US government’s latest U-turn on Anthropic’s Mythos sends mixed signals on AI governance
gist: The Trump administration's repeated flip-flopping on export controls for Anthropic's Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models — ban, partial release, expansion — reveals the US shifting from a deregulation posture toward volatile ad hoc control of frontier AI, which is bad for investors and undermines the international cooperation needed for global AI safety governance.

themes.theme: Timeline of the Mythos/Fable policy reversal

insights.claim: On 12 June 2026 the Trump administration issued an export control directive banning non-US nationals — including foreign employees at US companies and cyber defenders from international partners — from accessing Anthropic's Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models; Anthropic suspended access for all users the next day.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A blanket global suspension of a frontier model within 24 hours of a directive shows how quickly export-control decisions can disrupt commercial AI access and revenue, a volatility risk for Anthropic and enterprise customers to price in.
insights.locator: The move, described in a letter by Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnik

insights.claim: On 26 June 2026 the US government allowed Anthropic to release Mythos 5 but restricted access to a select group of 'trusted' big US companies and agencies only; by 1 July, Fable 5 (described as having stronger safeguards than Mythos) became available globally to public users; on 9 July (Tuesday) the Commerce Department fully removed restrictions on both models.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Three policy reversals in under a month on the same two models is a concrete case study of US frontier-AI export-control unpredictability that investors should factor into any AI-company or AI-policy-sensitive position.
insights.locator: On Tuesday, the United States Department of Commerce removed restrictions

insights.claim: Anthropic initially limited access to Mythos to a select group of trusted partners under 'Project Glasswing,' intended to let companies and agencies fix vulnerabilities in their systems and browsers, because of the model's powerful cyber hacking capabilities.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Frontier models are now explicitly being gated on offensive cyber capability, a governance pattern likely to recur for future model releases across the industry.
insights.locator: Since Anthropic's initial limited release of Mythos in April

themes.theme: US shift from deregulation to volatile ad hoc control

insights.claim: The US government is now regulating powerful AI in a way it had previously indicated it wouldn't, marking a significant shift from the Trump administration's general pro-deregulation stance driven by fear of stifling innovation and losing competitive edge over China.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A pivot from laissez-faire to interventionist AI policy under the same administration signals regulatory risk is rising for US AI companies even without a change in political party, a factor for AI-sector valuation and IPO timing.
insights.quote: the bigger picture is that the US government is regulating powerful AI in a way that it previously indicated it wouldn't
insights.locator: This latest U-turn on Mythos aside

insights.claim: The policy volatility around Mythos sends confusing signals to markets and is bad for investors, reflecting competing dynamics at the heart of US frontier AI strategy: anxiety about China's access to cutting-edge capabilities, lack of clarity on how transformative the technology actually is, and distrust in the partnerships needed to develop and deploy it.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Direct statement that this unpredictability is a negative signal for investors, useful shorthand for assessing regulatory risk premia on US frontier AI companies.
insights.quote: The policy volatility is concerning. Its unpredictability sends confusing signals to markets and is bad for investors.

insights.claim: OpenAI's latest models — GPT-5.6 Sol, Terra and Luna — have also come under government pressure due to security concerns and will initially be released only to a small group of trusted partners, reportedly at the request of the US government.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Shows the trusted-partner gating pattern is spreading beyond Anthropic to OpenAI, suggesting a broader industry-wide regulatory template rather than a company-specific dispute.

insights.claim: The Department of War (DoW) labelled Anthropic a 'supply chain risk' to national security earlier in 2026, the first US company ever to receive this designation, and remains in a legal battle with the company, even as Anthropic engineers reportedly help the NSA use Mythos in cyber operations against adversaries.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A frontier AI lab being simultaneously labeled a national security risk and used operationally by the NSA highlights deep internal contradiction in US government treatment of Anthropic, an idiosyncratic company-specific risk ahead of its expected IPO.
insights.locator: Earlier this year, the Department of War

insights.claim: Anthropic offered the EU access to Mythos after weeks of negotiations in early June 2026, only for the EU to lose that access days later when the 12 June export control directive hit, before presumably regaining it after the latest reversal; the G7 also saw attempts to renegotiate a 'trusted partners' scheme for cutting-edge AI access.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: US allies' AI access is being whipsawed by domestic US policy swings, a diplomatic friction point that could push allies toward accelerating their own sovereign AI capacity as a hedge.
insights.locator: In early June, Anthropic offered the EU access

insights.claim: Two AI companies central to this regulatory turbulence (Anthropic and OpenAI) are on the cusp of IPOs, making the unpredictable government relationship notable timing.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Regulatory volatility occurring just before major AI IPOs is a direct, timely watch item for public-market investors assessing IPO risk premium and pricing.
insights.locator: two of which are on the cusp of IPOs

insights.claim: A 2 June 2026 executive order called for AI companies to voluntarily submit models for safety testing for 30 days before general release, a period reportedly watered down from an original 90-day proposal after lobbying.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: The compressed testing window (90 to 30 days) after lobbying shows industry successfully softened this rule, a template for how future AI safety-testing requirements may be negotiated down.
insights.locator: On 2 June, an executive order called for AI companies

insights.claim: A 5 June 2026 national security directive instructed government agencies to end contracts with AI companies that limit how the government uses their technology, reportedly a response to the Pentagon-Anthropic legal battle.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Government procurement leverage is being used as a punitive tool against AI vendors that resist unrestricted government use of their models, a new form of policy risk for AI vendors selling into government.
insights.locator: On 5 June, a national security directive

insights.claim: Chatham House experts argue that tightening restrictions around valuable technology ('golden eggs' — software like models or hardware like chips) will not fully prevent their proliferation, and that clamping down on models immediately pre-release doesn't control or slow the frontier of development.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Challenges the efficacy premise of the whole export-control approach: restricting foreign access to already-developed frontier models is unlikely to meaningfully slow rival capability development, undermining the stated rationale for these controls.

insights.claim: Restricting foreign access to AI cyber capabilities, including for non-US AI safety institutes and allies, does not improve US readiness for an AI-enabled global crisis (such as a global financial crash) because it weakens the evidence base and trusted cooperation needed to navigate a shared shock.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Implies a hidden systemic-risk cost to export controls: excluding allies from AI safety information sharing could leave the global system less prepared for AI-driven crises, a tail risk relevant to financial stability monitoring.
insights.locator: clamping down on 'foreign access' to AI cyber capabilities

insights.claim: The inaugural UN Global Dialogue on AI Governance meeting faces an impossible balancing act because AI risks (to global health, nuclear security, financial systems) are shared and demand a minimum level of global governance including monitoring, information-sharing, model 'kill switches' and emergency backchannels — none of which is achievable without US and its AI companies' participation.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Global AI governance mechanisms (kill switches, emergency backchannels) that could matter for systemic financial and security risk are currently non-viable given unpredictable US frontier AI policy, meaning investors should not expect binding international AI safety guardrails soon.
insights.locator: Next week's inaugural United Nations meeting on AI

insights.claim: US-China dynamics in the AI race make it hard for Beijing and Washington to reach consensus on safety, despite promising signs of future intergovernmental talks.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Any near-term US-China AI safety cooperation should be treated as fragile and exploratory rather than a durable de-escalation signal.
insights.locator: This is complicated by the dynamics of the US-China AI race
